data.title: The Value Rotation Is Here: How To Position Your Portfolio For Maximum Yield
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Typically, we spend most of our time talking about individual picks in our public articles. Today, we are going to pull back the curtain and provide an article that is usually reserved for our members at High Dividend Opportunities, where we discuss our view of the macro environment and the trends in the market that are driving our decision-making about which sectors to look into for new opportunities and which positions we want to exit.

So, instead of focusing on exactly which picks to buy, let's talk about where the market is headed and where we want to look for dividends in 2026.

The Rotation to Value

It is still early, but so far, 2026 has been a year of rotation.

The iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) is up 6.3%, while the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF) is down 5.5%:

It's a remarkable shift from last year, where Growth was surging while Value stocks lumbered along.

One month doesn't make a trend, but there is a sense that the market is cooling on Growth and warming up to Value—but there are also some other notable movements in the market. Today, we want to take a look at the underlying drivers of the market and what that means for dividend investors.

An Emphasis on Safety

As we look at the strongest and weakest price action in our portfolio year-to-date, there appears to be a clear preference in the market for safety. Agency mREITs are up a lot to start out the year, even though they are trading at high premiums to book value.

Additionally, we've seen the discounts to NAV shrink for holdings like BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund, Inc. (DMB), which was trading at a 10% discount to NAV at the end of December and is now trading at just a 5% discount.

We see reliable dividend growers like Realty Income (O) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) seeing some positive momentum after a fairly flat 2025.

Essentially, the holdings in our portfolio that focus on safe credit assets or physical assets like commodities are performing the strongest.

On the other side of the coin, we are seeing credit risk holdings underperforming. In our portfolio, that primarily means BDCs and CLO funds have been flat to down.

While we continue to believe that credit losses are likely to remain relatively low, there is fear in the equity markets that is being reflected in the price action of businesses and funds that are exposed to credit risk.

This fear isn't being shared among institutions.

Public vs. Private Credit Concerns

Among many assets, there is a huge discrepancy between what the publicly traded stock market is willing to pay for assets and what institutions are willing to pay.

We've seen this in several scenarios in our portfolio. For example, SoftBank (SOBKY) is buying DigitalBridge (DBRG) and taking it private for over a 50% premium to its trading price before the rumor. Apollo Commercial (ARI) announced a deal to sell its entire loan portfolio for 99.7% of its carrying value, while the market was pricing in a 20% discount because it feared the credit risk. Even Realty Income has observed that private capital was willing to pay more for real estate assets and is opening up a fund that the public company will manage.

Here is a look at High Yield B Option-Adjusted Spreads. This is the spread that B-rated loans are getting over the benchmark; lower means that institutions are pricing in less default risk:

For some context, this is what this metric looked like going into 2008, before the S&P 500 started declining:

And here is what it looked like before the dot-com bust:

These spreads are primarily influenced by the supply and demand balance between lenders and borrowers. The B spreads are the most sensitive because it is a credit rating that implies higher risk, but it doesn't imply that there is distress going on right now. If there are a lot of lenders looking to deploy capital relative to companies looking to borrow, spreads will be lower, and they will accept a lower yield. If there are few lenders relative to the number of companies looking to borrow, then the spreads get higher. This is often indicative of either lenders having a lack of liquidity or lenders deciding that lending money to B credits is too high of a risk.

As a result, it can often serve as the canary in the coal mine, with spreads often widening considerably before a recession starts and even before the stock market declines.

These markets are dominated by institutions, whereas in the stock market, there is significant participation among retail investors. In the stock market, we're seeing many of our credit-risk-exposed holdings trading at steeper discounts or lower premiums than they have in the past.

Soft Fundamentals

As we've been documenting over the past several years, the fundamentals of the U.S. economy have been getting weaker. Unemployment has been rising steadily for three years:

Real Retail Sales (adjusted for inflation) have been relatively flat:

Consumers are feeling the squeeze with Real Personal Disposable Income growth slowing down:

Historically, this metric has averaged over 3%.

Consumer confidence continues to decline and, as measured by The Conference Board, is at COVID-era lows: Source

Where the Conference Board reported a sharp drop in January, a similar survey conducted by the UoM (University of Michigan) has a small uptick. However, the UoM survey was already far more bearish, with consumer sentiment near all-time lows: Source

Leading economic indicators continue to decline: Source

While there are undeniably some hotspots in the economy, like the huge investment being made in data centers, it is one hot sector that is surrounded by a lot of weakness.

Stock Picker's Market

The stock market, on average, is expensive. However, we have seen a diversion that hasn't occurred since the late 1990s, where the average P/E (price/earnings) ratio of the S&P 500 is materially higher than the median P/E ratio: Source

The median is the company that is in the middle, where 50% of companies in the S&P 500 are trading at higher valuations, and 50% are trading at lower valuations. We discussed the concept of valuation in-depth last month with our members because we believe this is the most important concept in the market today.

Microsoft (MSFT) reported objectively strong results, but the share price declined anyway. We've noticed that analysts are asking more questions about the profitability of AI spending rather than just blindly cheering it on. Here is a question that we think exemplifies this concern during the earnings call:

"Keith Weiss Morgan Stanley, Research Division

I'm looking at Microsoft print where earnings is growing 24% year-on-year, which is a spectacular result. Great execution on your part, top line growing well, margins expanding. But I'm looking at after-hours trading and the stock is still down. And I think one of the core issues that is weighing on investors is CapEx is growing faster than we expected and maybe Azure is growing a little bit slower than we expected. And I think that fundamentally comes down to a concern on the ROI on this CapEx spend over time. So I was hoping you guys could help us fill in some of the blanks a little bit in terms of how should we think about capacity expansion and what that can yield in terms of Azure growth going forward. More to the point, how should we think about the ROI on this investment as it comes to fruition?"

In essence, Wall Street is starting to say, "Show me the money!" There isn't much doubt about the potential for AI and that there will be tons of money to be made, but there are concerns about whether the money will show up in time to justify the trading prices for these stocks. Wall Street is no longer enraptured by the AI arms race; they are starting to ask for tangible results. If they don't find them, they'll move on to invest in sectors where tangible results are being realized right now. In a nutshell, that's exactly what the dot-com bust was all about. It wasn't that the Internet didn't have tons of potential; it was about whether that potential was translating into high enough earnings, soon enough, to justify the valuations today. In 2001, the answer was they weren't.

There are many companies that are trading at low valuations. The strength of the S&P 500 is increasingly relying on a handful of stocks to maintain very high valuations.

What This Means for Income Investors

Let's put the points we made together:

  • The market is favoring investments that are perceived as safer.
  • The public markets are valuing credit risk lower than private markets.
  • Economic fundamentals are soft, and consumers are struggling.
  • Some stocks are trading at premium valuations, while others are trading at low valuations.

Putting these together, what does that point to?

Hold Safety

We want to own assets that are perceived as "safe". Fortunately, we were buying a lot of those in recent years, like mREITs that invest in agency MBS, municipal bond funds, preferred equities, and bonds. We've been pounding the table until our hands were bruised about the virtues of fixed-income investments. We continue to believe that these are good investments, even if the prices are higher than they were. These investments are just starting to come back into favor and have some significant upside left to go.

For investors who don't have a healthy allocation to these investments, there is still time to add them.

Credit Risk Arbitrage Opportunities

For the credit risk portion of our portfolio, there is more opportunity, but also more risk. These investments are trading at lower valuations in the public markets than they can get in the private markets. As a result, the chances of seeing drastic moves like ARI's decision to sell substantially all of its assets are higher. We could see more of these publicly traded vehicles being taken private or large asset sales from publicly traded companies to private companies to take advantage of the valuation differences. The public companies will be exploring ways to unlock that value, which could create significant upside for public shareholders.

On the risk side, with investor enthusiasm low, prices are likely to remain under pressure. This will be accelerated to the extent that management teams reduce dividends in response to lower interest rates on floating-rate investments or as a strategy to retain capital to make new investments. We expect dividends for BDCs to generally drift downward toward 2021 levels, and CLO equity funds have become a wildcard with a big unknown on whether other funds will follow OXLC's example and position themselves to have a profile of growing NAV and paying lower dividends or if they will continue to pay out substantially all their cash flow at the expense of NAV.

We believe that long-term credit risk is trading at attractive valuations in the public markets. We believe that the institutions are right, and the risk of defaults is relatively low. But sentiment is negative, and it could become more negative as the year goes on.

Be Aware of the Consumer

When making an investment, we should be aware of whether our dividends are dependent upon a strong consumer. Dividends that stem from areas of relatively inelastic demand should be favored over dividends from sectors that are very elastic.

The Wendy's Company (WEN) is trading at a 7% yield, but it's a company that is quite dependent upon consumers being willing to pay for convenience. We expect another dividend cut to be very likely, so we will avoid it.

No doubt, we will see many consumer-centric businesses with yields that tip into our territory. We will exercise caution when choosing whether or not to take advantage of those "deals" because we recognize that the consumer is weak. We would rather own the landlord who rents properties to WEN because WEN is good for the rent even after they cut the dividend. O's 5% yield > WEN's 7%.

Be Aware of Valuations

When the market has been generally bullish for an extended period, there is often a tendency for investors to start dismissing the concept of valuation as irrelevant. It is true that buying something cheap doesn't mean that you'll make a lot of money right away. It doesn't mean that the price won't go lower for an extended period of time. However, in the long run, only two prices matter when calculating your total return: the price you pay and the price you sell at.

Prices bounce all over the place. Valuations go high, they dip low, and they will go to both extremes much further than any amount of staring at numbers can justify. We remember in 2021 when some preferred shares were trading at such high premiums to par that they had a negative yield-to-call, and people were buying preferred equity where the inevitable return was a guaranteed loss. There have been cases of bonds trading at negative yields to maturity.

If that kind of inaccurate pricing can occur in fixed income, where the amount of the interest/dividends is known to the penny, and you can calculate with a pencil and paper the best possible return that you can have by the call date or maturity date, and investors are still buying tickers that are guaranteed to have negative returns—how far off can the market be when the future returns are at best educated guesses that are open to debate? It can be off by a lot, and it can be off for a long time

Eventually, the chickens come home to roost, and the market comes around to recognize the earnings that it previously undervalued, or the earnings fail to materialize, and the price comes down to a level reflecting that. The thing is that there is always a future, so while the market will cross that "fair value" line, it will usually keep going until the extreme is in the opposite direction.

When a stock in your portfolio is up or down a lot, make sure you take the time to understand how much of that price difference is due to an actual change in earnings and how much is due to a change in valuation. You want to buy at a low valuation and consider selling when investments are trading at a high valuation.

Conclusion

It is a stock-pickers' market where there are some investments that are simply too expensive to be reasonable investments, some investments that are more expensive but still have a good long-term return profile, and some investments that are absolute bargains.

This is true for dividend investors as much as it is for everyone else. In our portfolio, we have a base with the relative safety of assets like agency MBS, fixed income, and bonds. For several years, this portion of our portfolio actually saw the lowest valuations. That is changing, although there are still opportunities that are attractive long-term.

To that base, we add investments in holdings and sectors that carry more risk but are trading at low valuations. Today, credit risk is a good place to look for investments that are trading at low valuations and paying very high yields.

We will focus on sectors that aren't dependent on a strong economy. Sectors supported by tangible assets and inelastic consumer demand. Real estate, utilities, infrastructure, and more.

We will realize gains when we believe that the trading price represents a valuation that is high, to free up capital to invest in opportunities that have similar or higher return potential.

data.title: Strategy: Time To Accumulate
data.description:
Investment Thesis

MicroStrategy (MSTR) is doubling down on its approach to accumulating more Bitcoin (BTC-USD) by utilizing structured digital credit to increase its amount of Bitcoin per share. The approach relies on controlled issuance of capital and an expectation that appreciation of Bitcoin will outpace costs of funding. In the near term, MSTR continues to be challenged by falling another 14% following a decline in Bitcoin. The main driver of MSTR’s direction remains appreciation of Bitcoin.

MSTR Targets 5%–14% Annual Bitcoin Yield via $6–10 Billion Digital Credit Issuance

I see Strategy holds a capacity to derive accretive value through the issuance of digital credit instruments, mainly like the Stretch (STRC) product. This operational function works by raising capital at a fixed cost and deploying the proceeds into Bitcoin, thereby increasing the Bitcoin/share metric.

MSTR raised over $25 billion in total capital during FY25. This capital inflow funded the acquisition of ~225,000 Bitcoin in FY25. The main metric tracking the efficacy of this operation is Bitcoin Yield that has hit 22.8% for FY25. This means that the holdings of Bitcoin per outstanding share increased by that percentage. It is outpacing the dilution caused by issuing new equity/credit instruments.

In my view, the Stretch product is the engine for this accretion. MSTR issues these preferred equity securities with a target dividend yield of 11.25% and a target price of $100. By paying a fixed 11.25% cost of capital whereas the underlying asset (Bitcoin) historically appreciates/is acquired at a rate that increases the per-share holding, MSTR captures the spread. Bitcoin is a 43% ARR asset in Strategy's view and the cost of the credit instrument is fixed.

This simple arbitrage permits MSTR to accumulate Bitcoin faster than it dilutes stockholding. The presence of $6.9 billion in preferred equity on the balance sheet marks a shift from convertible debt that carries maturity dates and repayment obligations to perpetual instruments that do not require principal repayment. I believe this structure removes the pressure of refinancing walls/redemption events and providing Strategy a permanent capital base for asset acquisition.

A main component of this model is the $2.25 billion cash reserve established in Q4-FY25. This reserve provides over 2.5 years of dividend coverage for the preferred equity and interest payments on convertible notes. So, the existence of this liquidity buffers the dividend obligations from the short-term price volatility of Bitcoin.

In my opinion, Strategy does not need to sell Bitcoin to service the 11.25% yield on STRC/the interest on convertible notes. This separation confirms that the Bitcoin holdings can compound over time. Strategy can utilize this cash reserve for any corporate purpose that includes satisfying credit obligations/paying dividends, despite market conditions.

The forward financial benefits from this structure is based on the continuous asset growth independent of operating profits from the software business. Strategy’s management projects selling $6 billion to $10 billion of digital credit annually over the next 7 years. In a low scenario involving $6 billion of annual sales, Strategy estimates a 5% annual Bitcoin Yield.

In a more aggressive scenario with lower interest rates allowing for 16%-20% digital credit sales, the model projects a 10%-14% annual Bitcoin Yield. This programmatic issuance ensures that the Bitcoin backing each share of MSTR stock increases. As the Bitcoin per share metric rises, the intrinsic value of the MSTR equity rises if Bitcoin price remains stable/increases over the long term.

Finally, the volatility stripping effect supports the valuation of the MSTR stock. How? By isolating the volatility into the equity tranche and offering a stable high-yield product to fixed-income market participants, MSTR branches out its capital structure. STRC absorbs capital seeking yield with low volatility (targeted at 7% volatility) and MSTR stock absorbs the amplified volatility and the accretive upside of the Bitcoin Yield.

This structure expands the pool of available capital and it is going beyond traditional equity buyers to include yield-focused fixed-income allocators. With the access of this deeper pool of capital at a fixed cost enables Strategy to scale its Bitcoin holdings more aggressively than if it relied solely on equity issuance/operational cash flow.

Bitcoin Must Exceed $76,000 to Reverse $17.5 Billion Losses and Restore Equity Value

As per my view, the core risk that Strategy is facing is the inversion between the average cost basis of its Bitcoin holdings and the market price of Bitcoin. MSTR holds 713,502 Bitcoins (714,644 as of now) with an average acquisition cost of $76,000 per Bitcoin.

However, the market price referenced in the Q4 is ~$60,000 to $69,900. This disparity means Strategy is deeply underwater on its main asset. The immediate result is a massive reported loss. In Q4 alone, MSTR recorded an operating loss of $17.4 billion and a net loss of $12.6 billion based on the mark-to-market decline in the fair value of its digital assets. The total unrealized loss on digital assets for Q4 stood at $17.5 billion.

I see this cost basis inversion as a severe drag on Strategy's GAAP financials as MSTR adopted fair value accounting. It means every downward fluctuation in Bitcoin price below the cost basis flows directly through the income statement as a loss. This results in negative EPS and obscures the operational performance of the legacy software business.

The valuation metrics reflect this strain. The Net Income Margin and ROE are negative and the P/E ratio is highly volatile and non-meaningful in trailing periods. I think a continued reporting of multi-billion dollar losses can restrict the institutional capital allocators to hold the stock.

Thus, the negative spread between cost and market value puts pressure on the book value of MSTR. Strategy essentially deployed $54 billion of capital to acquire assets that are now worth approximately $45 billion to $50 billion (based on the fluctuating prices mentioned above). This destruction of book value leverages the balance sheet negatively. The net leverage ratio is currently reported at 13%, but a further decline in Bitcoin price increases this leverage ratio relative to the asset base.

A decline to $8,000 per Bitcoin would equalize the asset value with the net debt and theoretically render the equity worthless in liquidation terms. Although this is an extreme scenario, in my opinion, the high cost basis of $76,000 still means that MSTR requires a considerable asset price recovery just to hit a breakeven point on its balance sheet capital allocation.

Looking forward, financial performance is hurt by the burden of servicing the capital used to acquire Bitcoin. Strategy has annual interest and dividend obligations totaling $888 million and this includes $713 million in dividend obligations from cumulative preferred stock and interest on convertible notes. The $2.25 billion cash reserve provides a mid-term buffer, but Strategy must derive cash to service these costs. The Bitcoin asset itself derives no organic cash flow.

Therefore, Strategy must depend on either the legacy software business that derives limited cash relative to the size of the treasury or the continuous issuance of new capital to pay for old capital. If the share price remains depressed due to the asset value being below the cost basis, this raising new equity option may become more dilutive and hit hard on the Bitcoin Yield metric.

Takeaway

Effectively, MicroStrategy is a leveraged compounding instrument in Bitcoin, and if the trend of Bitcoin is upwards, then the value of shares can be significantly magnified over time. However, the reverse is also true and for an investor, it’s a high-conviction, high-volatility play, but one that’s appropriate only if an investor is convinced that the long-term trend of Bitcoin is upwards.

data.title: Vertiv Holdings: Blowout Q4 Keeps The Bullish View Intact
data.description:
The Thesis

Exiting 2025, this leading AI infrastructure solutions provider, Vertiv Holdings (VRT), reported yet another strong quarter with double-digit expansion across the top and bottom lines, driven by robust AI-driven demand mainly across North America. As data center build-outs continue to accelerate, I expect demand for VRT's mission-critical power and thermal management solutions to remain healthy through 2026. Additional support from a massive backlog of approximately $15 billion should further fuel the company's consolidated topline growth in the quarters ahead.

Meanwhile, for margins, I believe strong pricing and operational efficiency should be key growth drivers as volume expands in the quarters ahead. The VRT stock has gained over 20% since my previous buy article, significantly outperforming the market. Although the stock continues to trade at a slight premium, its robust growth prospects and the company's dominant market position in the respective AI infrastructure space justify a bullish stance. While there might be some near-term volatility after the recent rally, given the company's promising long-term prospects, VRT stock remains a compelling buy for long-term investors.

VRT's Q4'25 Highlights

A few days back, VRT reported another blowout quarter as it exited FY2025. In the fourth quarter of the year, the company reported a solid 22.7% year-over-year growth in its consolidated topline, reaching a record $2.88 billion. The growth was yet again driven by a robust demand environment across the Americas region, which grew over 50% during the last quarter, with 46% organic growth.

While sales in the APAC region declined about 10% due to challenging macro in China, EMEA was down 8.2% due to soft demand across the region. For the full year 2025, VRT consolidated sales reached $10.2 billion, growing 27.7% as compared to the prior year.

While the momentum across the company’s topline remains strong, its profitability also continues to expand steadily due to volume leverage in the Americas segment. As pricing also continues to offset inflation's impact, the company’s adjusted operating margin expanded approximately 170 bps to 23.2% in the last quarter. Higher operating income also benefited the company’s bottom-line performance, as its adjusted EPS jumped nearly 38% to $1.36 versus the same quarter a year ago, surpassing the consensus estimates by $0.06 in Q4 2025.

Outlook

Throughout 2025, VRT has delivered exceptional growth and closed the year with another remarkable quarter, with solid double-digit expansion across its consolidated topline in Q4, thanks to robust momentum in its core Americas region. As AI-led data centers continue to accelerate across the region, I expect demand for VRT’s thermal and power management solutions to remain steady, primarily across North America, as the company enters into FY26.

The Americas, which contributes over 60% of the company’s consolidated revenue as of Q4, remains the largest and fastest-growing region. While AI-driven infrastructure build-outs continue to accelerate, the requirement for the company’s cooling architecture should remain strong.

This accelerating demand environment across North America was also reflected in the level of order activity in the last quarter, as total new organic orders in Q4 surged over 250% year-on-year, with an exceptional book-to-bill ratio of 2.9x. With such demand strength across the region, I believe the Americas segment will remain the key growth driver as VRT progresses through 2026.

Talking about APAC, the demand environment appears mixed in the short term, as China, which accounts for a meaningful share of the segment revenue, continues to face macroeconomic headwinds, which are likely to weigh on APAC performance in the quarters ahead. However, other parts of the regions, particularly India and Southeast Asia, continue to show strength due to rising AI infrastructure investments, which should largely offset the China headwinds over the coming quarters.

While order activity across EMEA showed significant improvement during Q4, the region continues to see a slower recovery, which could keep volumes under pressure in the short term. However, the bookings are expected to translate into sales growth in the latter half of FY26, which should support the company’s topline in the second half of the year.

Due to strong order activity throughout 2025, VRT's total backlog now stands at a whopping $15 billion, showing more than 100% growth as compared to a year ago. With a meaningful portion of this expected to be converted into sales within the next 12 months, revenue visibility remains meaningfully strong as the company progresses through FY26. In my view, while demand momentum in key regions is set to sustain, support from a massive order backlog positions VRT strongly to sustain double-digit topline growth in the quarters ahead.

Moving to the outlook beyond FY26, VRT’s story remains intact primarily due to secular tailwinds from AI-driven data center build-outs as hyperscalers across the regions continue to scale capacity with increasing compute capacity, which should keep demand for the company’s power and thermal solutions elevated beyond FY26.

The company has also introduced its prefabricated end-to-end data center infrastructure solutions, including Vertiv's OneCore and SmartRun. These solutions enable customers to deploy integrated power and thermal systems more efficiently and also enhance customer stickiness, which is likely to further benefit demand over the coming years.

Additionally, to further strengthen its fluid management capabilities, VRT recently acquired PurgeRite. This acquisition is expected to significantly enhance the company’s liquid cooling ecosystem and broaden its portfolio of advanced cooling technologies. While the company’s balance sheet remains strong with ample liquidity and a net leverage of just 0.5x, the company is well positioned to do more such strategic acquisitions over the coming years and strengthen its technological leadership and market reach, further supporting its topline growth in the longer term.

Valuation Update

I previously covered the VRT stock in October last year after it reported its Q3 earnings and maintained my bullish rating despite an expensive valuation, as growth prospects remained robust with no signs of slowing down. Since then, the stock has been very volatile. However, the stock still outperforms the market, especially due to the spike in the stock price after the blowout Q4 results last week. As a result, the VRT stock is currently up about 21% since my last buy rating, against a nearly flat growth in the S&P 500 during the same period.

Although the stock price has surged meaningfully since that article, interestingly, the valuation multiple remains nearly the same, thanks to multiple upward revisions in consensus EPS estimates. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E of 38.21, based on the FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $6.14 (earlier $5.19). When compared with its five-year forward average P/E of 27.6, the stock still looks overvalued. However, the premium appears to be still justified due to the company's strong market position and solid double-digit forward growth expectations, as can be seen below.

Moving ahead, Vertiv looks well-positioned to sustain the current topline growth momentum entering into FY26, as demand, particularly across North America, remains robust, and the order book remains at record levels. Meanwhile, with anticipated volume expansion, the company's inflation-beating pricing and gains from productivity should continue to support steady margin expansion in the quarters ahead.

Importantly, the company's debt levels remain significantly low, resulting in a significantly low-interest expenditure. So, I expect a large portion of operational gains and topline growth to translate into bottom-line growth over the coming quarters. Additionally, with continued outperformance, I expect more upward EPS revisions, especially post Q1 earnings release, which should further enhance the company's stock valuation in the future, supporting more upside.

Risks

Similar to my previous article, the stock's premium valuation remains the key risk to the bullish thesis. To justify and sustain the current multiple, VRT needs to continue to execute strongly and translate that into earnings growth over the coming quarter. Although the outlook appears solid entering into 2026, any signs of order moderation or slower-than-expected backlog conversion could impact growth in the short term.

Also, while India and a few other regions are showing strength in APAC, weakness in China and slower-than-expected recovery in EMEA regions might continue to weigh on consolidated growth. Overall, given the current valuation, expectations are undoubtedly high for VRT. So, any weakness or underperformance in the coming quarters could impact the stock performance badly in the future.

Final Verdict And Rating

As discussed above, despite the recent surge, the VRT stock continues to trade at a reasonable multiple. After a strong closing to FY25, VRT is expected to continue this momentum into FY26 with strong AI-driven demand and a robust backlog. While the company's long-term story also remains intact with secular tailwinds and its strong market position, I am staying bullish on this key AI infrastructure company at the present valuation. Hence, I am giving the VRT stock another “BUY” rating for investors with a long-term view.

data.title: 3 Once-In-A-Decade REIT Opportunities To Win Big
data.description:
Last week, I explained why I think that REITs are presenting a once-in-a-decade opportunity for investors to win big.

In short, they have been heavily discounted in recent years as all the attention was put on rapidly growing tech stocks.

However, I think that this is about to reverse.

The market is now finally waking up to the risk posed by AI to most businesses and their stocks.

This caused lots of tech stocks to crash in recent weeks. Those that were the most heavily impacted include SaaS (CRM; CNSWF; ROP; MNDY), Edtech (DUOL), Healthtech (HIMS), and Fintech (PYPL; COIN) companies:

The problem with these businesses is that they are purely digital, which makes them the easiest for AI to disrupt.

If before you needed $50 million and a team of 20 developers to create a competitor to a SaaS business, now you can do it with likely 10x less, thanks to AI. At the same time, a lot of the former clients of these businesses are likely to start developing company-internal tools themselves with the help of AI to save money.

The result is that the supply will surge, even as the demand will decrease. Some tech companies that enjoy significant switching costs will do okay in the short-to-mid-term, but over time, this will lead to lower retention rates, and that, on its own, could kill the long-term growth prospects and destroy a lot of value as this warrants a much lower valuation multiple.

AI is breaking barriers to entry and inviting new competition, which will lead to slower growth at minimum, and lower profitability at worst.

But guess what AI won't disrupt: essential real assets that our society desperately needs.

This is why REITs are the ultimate anti-AI asset class, and this is well-reflected in their recent performance as capital rotated from AI-disrupted businesses to anti-AI asset classes like REITs (VNQ):

My prediction is that this is just the beginning, and as more investors become worried about this value destruction, we will see a lot more capital rotate towards REITs, pushing them to a lot higher valuations.

And given that REITs are today still priced at near-decade low valuations, with many of them trading at steep discounts to their net asset values, the upside potential could be very significant.

In what follows, I am going to highlight 3 such once-in-a-decade opportunities to win big over the coming years.

Rayonier (RYN)

AI may disrupt a lot of SaaS companies, but guess what it won't disrupt: Timberland.

We are not making any more land, and timber will always remain a vital, environmentally friendly commodity that will be used for a lot of different things, ranging from construction to furniture, paper, packaging, and many other things.

Today, the average home is the oldest ever at 40 years old:

At the same time, we are facing an unprecedented housing affordability crisis, as too little has been built over the past decade:

Therefore, I think that it is an inevitability that the demand for timber will rise over time, as a lot of the existing housing stock needs to be rebuilt and/or remodeled, even as a lot more new housing is also needed to make up for the lack of sufficient construction of the past decade.

So the need for timberland and timber isn't going anywhere.

However, its supply is strictly limited and expected to even decline over time due to better-use conversions.

AI requires a ton of energy, and this is leading some timberland to be converted into renewable energy projects, especially in sun-rich regions.

At the same time, as AI accelerates the adoption of autonomous vehicles and remote work, I expect increasingly many people to move into more rural suburban locations where land is cheap and abundant. This will lead to even more timberland being converted into new housing projects.

Such better-use conversions from timberland into housing and renewable energy have historically yielded 5-15x value increases, resulting in large gains for Rayonier.

So long-term, I am really not worried about AI disrupting timberland. On the contrary, I think that it could benefit from it.

However, despite that, Rayonier (RYN), one of the leading timberland REITs, is today trading at a steep discount.

The REIT owns a vast portfolio of high-quality timberland and is backed by a conservative balance sheet with a 2.5x debt-to-EBITDA and a shareholder-friendly management team.

It is particularly heavily invested in the Southeast, which enjoys strong population growth, and much greater prospects for better-use conversion projects for solar and housing:

Yet, it trades at an estimated 30% discount to our estimate of its fair value, and offers a near 5% dividend yield.

It is also expected to enjoy significant synergies in 2026 and 2027 as it merges with its peer, PotlatchDeltic (PCH), increasing its cash flow and value.

This makes RYN a great pick for investors who worry about wealth preservation in an era in which AI is going to disrupt a lot of businesses.

The long-term upside is significant. It would need to rise by about 40% just to return to its net asset value, and we expect its net asset value to rise as well.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR)

What else is AI not going to disrupt? The need to store your company's goods before they are distributed.

On the contrary, I expect the demand for warehouses and distribution centers to only increase going forward, as AI is accelerating two important trends:

First, it supercharges e-commerce businesses. Their online marketing is becoming far more personalized and powerful. At the same time, it is also making it easier than ever before for anyone to start a new online shop. Therefore, we expect a boom in new small e-commerce business formation, even as the existing ones will keep growing and stealing market share from traditional retailers. This is expected to result in a lot more demand for these properties.

Second, AI and robotics will also help to close the cost gap between the US and developing countries, which will lead to more companies bringing back larger portions of their supply chains to the US.

Both of these trends could greatly boost the demand for industrial real estate over the coming years.

But what's the best industrial REIT to buy today?

I think that Rexford Industrial is one of the best opportunities for two reasons.

First, it is one of the few industrial REITs that is still heavily discounted, trading at a steep discount to its net asset value and its peer groups:

The reason why it is discounted is that it is currently dealing with some oversupply, which is slowing down its near-term growth prospects. On top of that, it is selling some assets to pay debt, further reducing its growth potential.

As a result, its cash flow is expected to stagnate in 2026 and only return to slow growth in 2027.

But here's why I think that this is a great opportunity.

Historically, REXR has typically traded at a premium relative to its close peers because it focuses on some of the world's supply-constrained markets in Southern California. It is and will always remain an important strategic location for export/import business, right by the Pacific and the Mexican border.

These natural obstacles limit supply growth and have historically resulted in above-average cash flow growth:

Rexford has suffered some oversupply in the post-pandemic world, as the near-0% interest rates led to an unusually large wave of new supply. Moreover, many tenants overestimated their near-term needs and ended up taking too much space, some of which they then later vacated.

But things are now gradually normalizing, and the long-term prospects remain very compelling for these assets. REXR also has nearly 10% of vacant space that's waiting to be released, and could enjoy significant upside potential as it returns closer to its net asset value.

The legendary activist investment firm, Elliott, recently initiated a position and has been pushing the management to unlock value by selling assets and buying back shares. As a result, they bought back $250 million of stock at an average price of $39.51 in 2025.

Today, the share price is even lower, offering a near 5% dividend yield, and trading at an estimated 30% discount to net asset value. Just to return to its NAV, its stock would need to rise by about 40%, and its cash flow has significant long-term upside potential on top of that.

Kimco Realty (KIM)

Another thing that won't get disrupted any time soon: going grocery shopping at Sprouts (SFM), getting a haircut, going to the dry cleaner, and grabbing some food at the drive-through of McDonald's (MCD).

Kimco is one of the leading strip center REITs, focusing on grocery-anchored and service-oriented properties that are e-commerce resistant.

This property sector is doing very well today. Retail has been out of favor for a long time, and as a result, very little was built, and it has now become undersupplied, with occupancy rents rising to near all-time highs and steady 3-5% annual same-property NOI growth.

In fact, Kimco's occupancy rate just hit all time highs, and its FFO per share grew by 6.7% in 2025:

This is unlikely to change any time soon, given that rents remain far too low relative to construction costs. According to Kimco, rents would need to rise by 65% on average for new development projects to become economically feasible in its markets:

Kimco also has an A-rated balance sheet, a rarity in the REIT sector.

Despite that, it trades at a historically low valuation.

Its stock is so cheap, in fact, that the management has been steadily buying back shares lately.

It today offers the fastest growth at the lowest multiple in its property sector:

We estimate that it trades at a 25% discount to its net asset value and offers a near 5% dividend yield.

We think that its net asset value also presents upside as its NOI rises and cap rates compress in this property sector.

All in all, the stock could return 50%+ in the next few years as it returns near its NAV.

Investor Takeaway

A lot of REITs are still trading at 30%+ discounts to their net asset value.

What this essentially means is that you get to buy high-quality real estate at 70 cents on the dollar and then get the added benefits of liquidity, diversification, and professional management on top of that.

Historically, whenever REITs have been this cheap, they have generated very strong returns in the following years:

And with increasingly many investors now becoming worried about AI-driven disruption to businesses, I expect REITs to soon regain popularity and recover.

We are positioning our portfolio accordingly by investing heavily in undervalued REITs at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: ClearBridge International Value Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Participating Well in Value Tailwinds

Market Overview

International equity markets generated strong returns in the fourth quarter, capping a year marked by shifting leadership and unusually wide style dispersion. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index rose 4.9% in the quarter to finish up 31.2% for 2025, outperforming the S&P 500 Index’s gains of 2.7% for the quarter and 17.9% for the year. International value had an even stronger year, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index returning 42.2%, handily outpacing its growth counterpart by over 2,100 bps, the widest dispersion among investment styles in the 50-year history of the index.

Exhibit 1: International Value Growth Dispersion at All-Time Highs

As of Dec. 31, 2025. Source: ClearBridge Investments.

In the fourth quarter, market narratives remained heavily focused on artificial intelligence-related investment, reflected most visibly in the outsize performance of technology-heavy markets such as Taiwan and South Korea. However, the quarter also saw continued strength across emerging markets, commodities and select value-oriented sectors, underscoring a gradual broadening in market participation. A weaker U.S. dollar and expectations for easier monetary policy helped support sentiment toward emerging markets and consumer-sensitive areas.

"Leadership broadened beyond U.S. mega cap growth, creating a favorable backdrop for international value."

From a macroeconomic perspective, growth continued to slow in Europe, particularly across manufacturing-related industries, though services activity remained resilient and equity markets generally held up well. In China, signs of stabilization in manufacturing activity supported risk appetite, while the U.S. consumer remained comparatively resilient. Despite the “everything rally” that characterized much of 2025, the fourth quarter highlighted how expectations, positioning and valuation continue to play an outsize role in driving relative outcomes.

Exhibit 2: MSCI Growth vs. Value Quarterly Performance

As of Dec. 31, 2025. Source: FactSet.

Quarterly Performance

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, driven by stock selection in information technology (IT) and industrials, but partially offset by financials. Our positioning in companies aligned with long-term structural themes — such as digital AI infrastructure, the energy transition and capital markets normalization — proved especially beneficial.

IT was the largest contributor to relative performance. In South Korea, SK Hynix (HXSC.F) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), leading semiconductor manufacturers, benefited from higher memory prices and strong pricing and volume growth as global data center buildouts accelerated, driving meaningful earnings momentum across the memory complex. In Japan, Fujitsu (FJTSF), an IT services and solutions provider, advanced as corporate digitization efforts and labor shortages drove demand for efficiency-enhancing technologies.

Industrials also contributed, supported by long-term infrastructure and electrification themes. Siemens Energy (SMEGF), a German manufacturer of power generation and transmission equipment, continues to benefit from rising demand for grid upgrades and gas turbines as utilities expand electricity generation capacity to meet data center power needs. Offsetting some of these gains was CNH Industrial (CNH), a global manufacturer of agricultural and construction equipment, which detracted as elevated channel inventories and depressed farm incomes weighed on near-term results; however, we remain encouraged by management’s efforts to improve profitability through the cycle.

Stock selection in the financials sector weighed on relative performance. Rakuten Bank (RKUNY), a Japanese digital banking platform, declined as higher deposit acquisition costs weighed on near-term profitability, despite our view that these investments will ultimately support a sticky customer base and benefit from rising domestic interest rates. However, these headwinds were partially offset by Lloyds Banking Group (LYG), a U.K.-focused retail and commercial bank, which performed well as investor concerns around budget-related risks proved overdone and investor focus returned to the company’s strong earnings visibility and capital return profile.

From a regional perspective, relative performance was supported by strong results in Europe ex U.K., reflecting favorable stock selection amid continued investment in infrastructure and electrification. Emerging markets also proved positive, with strength in our Korean holdings offsetting profit taking in China-related exposures, including Tencent (TCEHY), which we believe will continue to be a significant beneficiary of the buildout in AI applications. Meanwhile, stock selection in Japan detracted, as gains in technology-related holdings were offset by weakness in interest-rate-sensitive areas, including Daito Trust Construction (DIFTY), which underperformed as higher policy rate expectations and cost inflation pressured margins.

Portfolio Positioning

During the quarter, we initiated several new positions that reflect our focus on improving fundamentals at attractive valuations.

We established a position in Roche (RHHBY), a Switzerland-based global biopharmaceutical and diagnostics company. Positive Phase III data across multiple late-stage programs meaningfully mitigates upcoming patent expirations later this decade, while mid-single-digit revenue growth, an improving pipeline in areas such as obesity and Alzheimer’s, and a discounted valuation relative to peers support our view that the stock offers rerating potential as execution improves.

We also initiated a position in Merck KGaA (MKGAF), a Germany-based science and technology company with businesses spanning life sciences, health care and electronics. While portions of its health care segment have faced near-term revenue pressure, recent acquisitions and a deep pipeline offer longer-term upside, and we believe the market is underappreciating a cyclical recovery in its life sciences and electronics businesses as order trends stabilize and management confidence improves.

We exited Gerresheimer (GRRMF), a German manufacturer of specialty packaging for the pharmaceutical and cosmetics industries, after losing confidence in management’s ability to execute a turnaround following repeated profit warnings and operational missteps. We believe capital is better deployed in opportunities with clearer pathways to improvement.

Outlook

We enter 2026 with a more stable macro environment than this time last year. Inflation has moderated globally, giving central banks room to ease, while fiscal programs — from U.S. industrial and infrastructure spending to expanded European budgets and targeted Chinese stimulus — continue to support economic activity. With the effective U.S. tariff rate already having peaked, companies that absorbed tariff-related cost pressures in 2025 should lap those headwinds, creating modest tailwinds for growth.

Several themes are likely to shape markets in 2026:

A less synchronized monetary backdrop: The global monetary backdrop is increasingly mixed rather than uniformly accommodative. While selective rate cuts may emerge in regions such as the U.K. and parts of Europe, other central banks — including Japan and Australia — appear more inclined toward tightening or maintaining restrictive settings, and the path for U.S. policy remains uncertain. In this environment, growth outcomes are likely to be shaped less by synchronized easing and more by regional and sector-specific dynamics, with rate-sensitive housing, utilities and infrastructure benefiting where policy flexibility exists and fiscal support and structural investment sustaining activity elsewhere.

Leadership expands beyond mega cap AI: While AI remains foundational, power, logistics and efficiency improvements are becoming equally important investment themes. Companies that enable the next phase of the AI cycle — rather than those solely capturing its front-end demand — are well-positioned. Regionally, China continues to build out its own AI infrastructure. As companies there are more limited in resources, they must look to quickly monetize their investments. Companies like Tencent are finding ways to profit from these investments now but still continue to trade at large discounts to similarly positioned companies in the U.S.

Emerging markets retain meaningful value: Although outside our benchmark, EM remains one of the more attractively valued areas globally, trading at a roughly 40% discount to the U.S. Disinflation offering monetary flexibility, countries like Brazil and Mexico on firmer fiscal footing and an easing dollar should support flows, bolster returns and create a more fertile ground for potential alpha generation.

The U.K. looks compelling: Attractive valuations, improving inflation dynamics and falling gilt yields have created a supportive backdrop — particularly for its concentration of service-oriented industries that should benefit from AI and are spared tariff headwinds and threats of excess capacity of Chinese exports.

M&A could provide an additional tailwind: Deregulation, strategic repositioning and the prospect of lower interest rates may support an uptick in M&A globally. Companies will likely act more decisively in an environment with reduced policy uncertainty.

With a more balanced macro backdrop, healthier geographic diversification and an expanding set of fundamental catalysts, 2026 presents a more attractive opportunity than the narrowly led markets of recent years. The companies best positioned from here are those driving meaningful internal financial and operational improvements that can support long-duration value creation.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy outperformed its MSCI EAFE benchmark during the fourth quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had gains across nine of the 11 sectors in which it was invested, with the IT and financials sectors the greatest contributors and the communication services sector the largest detractor.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection effects contributed positively to outperformance. In particular, stock selection in the IT, industrials and materials sectors and an underweight to the communication services sector proved beneficial. Conversely, stock selection in the financials and health care sectors and an underweight to the health care sector weighed on performance.

On a regional basis, stock selection in Europe Ex U.K. and strategic suballocations to South Korea within emerging markets, as well as an underweight to Asia Ex Japan, benefited performance. An overweight to North America and stock selection in Japan weighed on performance.

On an individual stock basis, SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Fujitsu, Lloyds Banking and Siemens Energy were the greatest contributors to relative performance. The largest relative detractors were Rakuten Bank, CNH Industrial, Tencent, Daito Trust Construction and a Roche underweight.

During the quarter, in addition to the transactions mentioned above, the Strategy inherited a position in Magnum Ice Cream in the consumer staples sector following its spinoff from holding Unilever and initiated a new position in Brookfield Renewable in the utilities sector. We exited positions in Sony Financial and JTC in the financials sector, Akeso and Hikma Pharmaceuticals in the health care sector and COVER in the communication services sector.

Sean Bogda, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Grace Su, Portfolio Manager

Jean Yu, CFA, Ph.D., Portfolio Manager

data.title: Credo Technology: The AI Energy Efficiency Architect
data.description:
Investment thesis

Not only does the AI industry require scaling and increasing the computing power of data centers, it also requires solutions to ensure stable data transmission and energy efficiency. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) is one of the few unique players in the AI market offering semiconductors that solve hyperscalers' problems in developing network infrastructure. This trend, observed in 2025, most likely will continue in 2026. CRDO not only offers existing technologies but also continues to improve them, further increasing data transfer speeds and reducing energy consumption in computing processes for its customers. I would like to take a closer look at the company's growth drivers, the competition it faces from other companies, as well as the risks that may arise when investing in its shares. Given the results, however, I believe that CRDO's stock still has significant growth potential (potential growth of 20% in 2026), and therefore my recommendation for this asset is "Buy."

CRDO's strategy and growth drivers

Established in 2008, today Credo plays an increasingly important role in the development of the AI industry, delivering reliable server connectivity to computing clusters and data center operators. A fabless semiconductor company, it offers technology solutions for high-speed data transmission. CRDO's main goal is to solve the problem of bandwidth bottlenecks in data centers by offering increased data transfer speeds and making computing processes more energy efficient. These technological advantages allow the company to sign contracts with major hyperscalers, optical module manufacturers, and network equipment manufacturers.

Credo's investment appeal stems not only from its focus on AI and ensuring the energy efficiency of data centers, maar also from the company's transition to new standards and products. These include, for example, the 6.0 retimers (Toucan series) and DSP ZeroFlap 1.6T, both of which deliver ultra-high data transfer speeds. Building new data centers faces two barriers: with the need to transfer large amounts of data between thousands of GPUs and with energy saving requirements at all levels of digital infrastructure (given the shortage of electricity and its rising cost). Tech solutions from Credo make computing processes in data centers faster and less energy-intensive.

Under this strategy, Credo has developed three product lines. SerDes IP and chiplet portfolio includes the supply of individual chiplets required for the construction of specialized HPCs. Also included is the licensing of high-speed interface technologies to other chip manufacturers. Credo's Active Electrical Cables portfolio is its flagship product, featuring a new type of electrical cable with built-in signal recovery chips. Such cables are important because they offer three advantages: ils are thinner, lighter, and, most importantly, use almost 50% less energy. Active electrical cables play an important role in creating the most energy-efficient network infrastructure when building AI servers. The third portfolio of Credo is optical digital signal processors, consisting of the production of microchips necessary for optical transceivers. Currently, in addition to actively implementing standard 400G and 800G solutions, it is also working on transitioning to the latest 1.6T.

Competition for CRDO

Analysing the competitive environment for CRDO, note that in 2026 it will be characterised by intensifying competition between different groups of manufacturers. Several companies offer similar solutions for AI clusters and data center operators. Additionally, because CRDO specializes in niche technologies, competitors that similarly specialize in the same solutions/products should be highlighted separately.

On the one hand, it competes with direct rivals that specialize in data transmission infrastructure. I think Astera Labs (ALAB) is the main competitor. They're a big deal because they're the top dog in the PCIe retimer segment. Parallel to this, the CRDO has a competitive advantage in the AEC cable segment. Starting in 2025, the ALAB and CRDO began to encroach on each other's territory, intensifying competition between them for space on AI server boards. We can also highlight Alphawave Semi as a competitor, as it offers highly efficient solutions for interfaces and IP blocks. In addition, this company competes with CRDI in the technology licensing and chiplet development segment.

Furthermore, the CRDO is competing with major companies in the semiconductor industry. Such players as Marvell Technology (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), and NVIDIA (NVDA) offer comprehensive network solutions. The main competitive advantage of these companies is their access to vast resources and an established customer base, enabling them to find new orders with less time expenditure. Among these three major players, particular attention should be paid to MRVL, since it is the main strategic competitor for CRDO. Within the framework of Marvell's strategy, it is developing the Golden Cable initiative, which aims to make the company the market leader in AEC cables. With its strong portfolio of optical DSPs and existing long-term contracts with hyperscalers such as Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), MRVL offers competitive solutions for ensuring the energy efficiency of network infrastructure. As an example, NVIDIA promotes its proprietary standards, whereby all solutions must originate internally. There is a risk that a company such as NVDA will cease to purchase third-party technologies from CRDO. Meanwhile, Broadcom is focused on the switch segment, with DSP solutions and SerDes tech. Although they're pretty standard and geared toward the upper level of the network hierarchy, they offer solutions for improving energy efficiency that reduce demand for Credo's tech.

Finally, we have a market of specialized players that focus exclusively on specific components used in network infrastructure development solutions. Examples include companies such as Semtech (SMTC) and MaxLinear (MXL), both of which compete with CRDO in the DSP segment by providing increased signal transmission speeds over short distances. Others, including Coherent (COHR) and Lumentum (LITE), are offering alternative solutions for the DSP segment, while also being partners with Credo to some extent. To sum up the competitor analysis, we would highlight two of the most dangerous players: ALAB and MRVL. One of Astera Labs' strengths is its deep integration with the PCIe and CXL ecosystems. At Marvell, its competitive advantages are its scale with access to capital, its broad portfolio ranging from the ability to offer unique technology solutions to established long-term contracts. But while ALAB has a weakness due to its dependence on the pace of data bus standard updates, at MRVL it's because of its higher inertia.

CRDO Financial Profile

The financial profile of CRDO's business appeals to me because the company is demonstrating positive momentum, demonstrating that it is capable of not only spending investor capital but also generating profits. For Q2 of FY2026, non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.67, up $0.17 from the consensus estimate. Revenue, in turn, was up 272.1% to $268.03 million (exceeding forecasts by $33.04 million). Meanwhile, free cash flow was $38.5 million, with cash and cash equivalents at $813.6 million.

Awaiting quarterly results for Q3 of FY 2026

CRDO plans to publish its next quarterly results on March 2, 2026. Analyst consensus forecasts expect EPS of $0.94, down 274.32% from the same period a year earlier. The revenue is forecasted to be $385.94 million, an increase of 185.88% over the same period. In the last 3 months, analysts on Wall Street have revised their EPS and revenue forecasts 12 times, each time raising their estimates. I believe that the probability of seeing data better than the forecast is quite high, because in the last eight quarterly reports, there was only one negative surprise. Moreover, CRDO's management recently raised its forecasts for Q3 of FY 2026, anticipating revenue of $404 million to $408 million, up from the previous forecast of $335 million to $345 million.

CRDO valuation

In order to assess the potential value of CRDO, we must take into account that using traditional P/E multiples is not reasonable, since the company has enormous R&D and scaling costs. As a result, other multiples should be used. An example is the EV/Sales valuation. The company's revenue forecast for 2026 is currently $1.32 billion. Therefore, its projected EV/Sales for 2026 will be 16.1x. Looking at the EV/Sales values of its main competitors, the multiples are higher. For example, the highest multiple is 24.0x for ALAB. The market values AVGO at 25.0x, reflecting an additional premium for stability and dominance in the custom chip market. With a revenue growth rate lower than Credo's, MRVL has a significantly cheaper multiple of 9.1x. Taking their median, which is 19.3x, if we revalue the shares to the median EV/Sales multiple, CRDO's value would increase by 20% from the current $121.44 to $145.72.

CRDO Risks

The main risk for investing in CRDO shares, as I see it, comes from the high concentration of customers, meaning that its future financial results are totally dependent on a small number of companies. In addition, even though Credo offers unique solutions that increase data transfer speeds and do so with half the energy consumption, the company's valuation appears aggressive (given its 72.3% growth over the past 12 months), due to the influence of hype and irrational optimism surrounding the ongoing AI supercycle.

In terms of high customer concentration, CRDO's order portfolio depends on several hyperscalers. The management clarified during the last conference call that the four largest customers account for 42%, 24%, 16%, and 11% of revenue. In the past, working with Amazon alone brought in up to half of the company's total revenue. Therefore, it's possible that if AMZN develops solutions that increase data transfer speeds and reduce energy consumption without Credo's products, the company's stock price could drop significantly, like it did in Q1 2023. Also negatively affecting the valuation is the fact that the orders themselves are binary in nature, arriving in waves. This makes it critically important for Credo to meet order deadlines, because otherwise it will lead to a lack of new orders.

Additionally, I mentioned in the competition section that a big challenge for CRDO is pressure from other companies that are way more tech-savvy and have more capital and access to funding. In particular, development of the MRVL "Golden Cable" concept could lead to Credo losing a significant market share. Moreover, the testing of AVGO’s own switches could reduce the need for certain Credo chips, thereby impacting financial results. In general, we should not forget that every major hyperscaler conducts its own internal development, reducing its dependence on third-party solutions and external suppliers. With each successful project, there is less potential for Credo to sign new contracts with network infrastructure operators.

Last but not least, it is important to remember the risk of a continuing AI supercycle, the termination of which could have negative consequences for all players in the network economy. Nevertheless, it is my opinion that this risk will diminish in 2026 due to CAPEX growth, whereas many other analysts may perceive this to be a warning sign of potential threats and a future crisis. Market participants are currently concerned that CAPEX will increase sharply in 2026, because the economic return on investment in AI infrastructure development has not been fully proven. Here's a look at how CAPEX is expected to grow in 2026 compared to 2025 for the largest hyperscalers.

Ultimately, the total CAPEX in 2026 will be around $750 billion, up $350 billion from 2025 and $510 billion from 2024. But this continued investment in AI development will most likely lead to the expansion of clusters, requiring more technology and solutions to ensure high-speed data transfer and reduce the energy consumption of data centers. This could mean that all those billions invested in CAPEX in 2026 could be a positive stimulus for increased demand for Credo products.

Conclusion

As a result, a fundamental analysis of Credo shows that its shares are highly attractive for investment. A key factor in continued growth will be the CAPEX of hyperscalers, as they increase their spending on semiconductors. Credo's technology solutions enable high-speed data transmission, allowing data centers to eliminate bandwidth bottlenecks, speed up data transfer, and make computing processes more energy efficient. These technological advantages enable the company to sign contracts with major hyperscalers, optical module manufacturers, and network equipment manufacturers. For this reason, investment in CRDO in 2026 appears to be an attractive proposition, in particular if players such as Amazon and Microsoft continue to purchase cables from them for their AI clusters, given the absence of cheaper and higher-quality alternatives on the market.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Masimo Jumps As Danaher Nears $10B Deal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Danaher (DHR) nears $10B acquisition of medical tech company Masimo (MASI), FT reports. (00:14) Elliott builds over 10% stake in Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH) to push changes - report. (01:08) Costco (COST) sued over rotisserie chicken as nonprofit alleges salmonella at Nebraska plant. (02:05)

This is an abridged transcript.

Masimo (MASI) is up 32% in premarket action.

Danaher (DHR) is nearing a roughly $10 billion deal to acquire the medical technology firm.

The Financial Times reported, citing people familiar with the matter, a deal could be announced as early as today, provided it does not hit any last-minute snags, the report said.

That deal value represents a premium to its nearly $7B market capitalization at Friday’s close.

Masimo manufactures pulse oximeters, which measure blood oxygen levels. The company has challenged Apple over breaching its patents in a long-running intellectual property dispute over the Apple Watch.

The Masimo deal would be Danaher’s (DHR) biggest acquisition in more than half a decade since its $21.4B takeover of Cytiva, the biologics manufacturing arm previously owned by GE Life Sciences, the report said.

Masimo’s (MASI) stock has declined by 27% over the past year.

Danaher is down 4% premarket.

Activist investor Elliott Investment Management has bought over a 10% stake in Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH), positioning it as one of the company's largest shareholders.

WSJ reported, citing sources familiar with the matter, that Elliott intends to advocate for major operational and strategic changes to address underperformance.

The cruise company has lagged competitors, with its shares down nearly 4% YTD, compared to gains of 4.5% for Carnival (CCL) and 15% for Royal Caribbean (RCL).

Norwegians shares sank on weak Q3 results and forecasts amid worrying cruise industry trends.

To note, the company recently appointed John W. Chidsey, former CEO of Subway and Burger King, as president and CEO effective February 12, 2026, replacing Harry Sommer. Chidsey, a board member since 2025, brings experience in consumer brand turnarounds.

Costco (COST) is reportedly facing a new lawsuit over its rotisserie chicken.

The proposed class action lawsuit filed on Thursday cited a December study from an animal rights nonprofit alleging that the company’s Nebraska chicken processing plant has ongoing salmonella contamination issues.

"Costco’s failure to control salmonella in its chicken supply is not a harmless technicality—it poses a real danger to consumers and violates their trust," the complaint said, according to a Reuters report.

The plaintiff, Lisa Taylor, of Affton, Missouri, said she routinely bought one or two rotisserie chickens a month at Costco warehouses in the St. Louis area and believes she overpaid because Costco didn't disclose the contamination risk, the report said.

Costco said at its annual meeting last month that $4.99 rotisserie chicken sales topped 157 million worldwide in 2025.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

data.title: PDI: A Special Entry Point For This Fund
data.description:
Understanding the investing cycle is important. While obviously long-term investing goals are by definition inflexible, the strategies used to reach these benchmarks should change as the market environment evolves. Certain investment strategies, such as focusing on the energy sector, worked well during the recent inflationary period, but these approaches are obviously no longer as effective in the current economic landscape.

The rate of inflation has consistently remained below 3 percent for a while now. January's consumer price index report showed inflation levels falling to 2.4 percent, which was near a 5-year low. With the US economy now clearly in an economic period of slowing inflation, certain types of investments are likely to outperform. One of those financial options is leveraged funds focused on fixed- and variable-rate bonds, such as the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI).

Today, I am initiating coverage of the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund with a buy rating. This leveraged bond fund has a diverse and well-constructed portfolio that should benefit from what should be lower rates moving forward when Powell is replaced in the middle of this year. This investment should see several key advantages from the current, more dovish rate cycle that the market is likely to enter into for an extended period of time.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has $7.47 billion in assets under management, an expense ratio of 1.64 percent, interest expenses of 2.76 percent, and a forward yield of 14.22 percent. PDI's five largest bond holdings are Freddie Mac season loans, Promotora De Informaciones, JP Morgan Securities, Amsurg Corporation, and US Renal Care. These holdings make up nearly 11 percent of the overall fund's positions.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's investment holdings are heavily weighted to fixed and higher-rate mortgage-backed securities that make up just under half of the closed-end fund's assets. PDI's bond portfolio is composed primarily of BB and below-rated fixed-income instruments that mature in 1-5 years. The fund has nearly 41 percent of its assets invested in bonds that are below B-rated.

PDI is more vulnerable to economic and rate cycles because the fund holds a significant amount of junk bonds, and most of the fund's positions in the bond market are in fixed-rate positions.

PDI only sold off 15 percent during the pandemic, and the CEF was also able to maintain monthly payouts even in that more difficult economic environment. While the fund's distributions during COVID were frequently done with return of capital, which caused the net asset value to decline by nearly 30 percent, PDI maintained the same payout rate during the pandemic, and the fund did not experience alarming default rates in the bond portfolio at that time.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's distribution has been very consistent over the last decade, and the CEF has not had to rely on return of capital recently.

Since 2022, PDI has relied heavily on the return of capital one year to make distributions in 2024. While the net asset value has fallen from nearly $22.59 in 2020 to nearly $17 today, the NAV has remained stable over the last 3 years.

The fund is also trading at just a 10 percent premium to the net asset value, even though the current likely extended rate cycle of lower interest rate levels is much more favorable for this investment.

The consensus economic outlook is for growth to remain slow but stable. Goldman Sachs recently raised the firm's forecast for the US economy in 2026, which is for nearly 2.8 percent GDP growth, with the bank projecting lower tax rates and falling core inflation as economic catalysts for this year. Trump will also be replacing Powell in May, and the Central Bank is likely to be more dovish moving forward, with core inflation expected to ease.

All investments have risks, and if core inflation were to accelerate and the Fed had to reverse course, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's valuation would likely contract significantly for a number of reasons. The significant junk bond holdings of PDI would also obviously be at added risk if the economy were to weaken significantly, given most of the shorter-term maturities of this fund's assets.

PDI weathered the storm over the last several years, and the fund is now well-positioned to deliver what should be consistent and substantive income for some time. Lower rates should benefit this PIMCO fund in a variety of ways as well, since mortgage financing rates would fall and the cost of the investment's leverage would decrease as well. Investors will likely be more willing to pay more for this investment in that environment, and the net asset to share price premium should rise in the coming years.

data.title: Artisan Value Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Process

We seek to invest in companies that are undervalued, in solid financial condition and have attractive business economics. We believe that companies with these characteristics are less likely to experience eroding values over the long term.

Attractive Valuation

We value a business using what we believe are reasonable expectations for the long-term earnings power and capitalization rates of that business. This results in a range of values for the company that we believe would be reasonable. We generally will purchase a security if the stock price falls below or toward the lower end of that range.

Sound Financial Condition

We prefer companies with an acceptable level of debt and positive cash flow. At a minimum, we seek to avoid companies that have so much debt that management may be unable to make decisions that would be in the best interest of the companies’ shareholders.

Attractive Business Economics

We favor cash-producing businesses that we believe are capable of earning acceptable returns on capital over the company's business cycle.

Team Overview

Everyone on the team functions as a generalist with respect to investment research and the entire team works together on considering potential investments.
