Investing Environment

US equity markets continued to advance in Q4 2025, despite bouts of volatility. Early in the quarter, a government shutdown unsettled investors as delays in key economic data releases complicated the market’s ability to assess underlying growth and inflation trends, raising doubts about the timing and durability of further Federal Reserve easing. As the quarter progressed, however, risk appetite improved alongside greater clarity around monetary policy. The Fed implemented additional rate cuts and formally concluded quantitative tightening, reinforcing expectations that financial conditions would continue to ease into 2026.

Markets also digested a shifting policy backdrop on trade as court challenges renewed uncertainty around the scope and durability of existing tariffs. While these developments created episodic volatility, they did little to derail broader equity momentum. Enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI) remained a central driver of performance, though investor focus began shifting from early-stage adoption narratives toward questions of monetization, competitive intensity and the sustainability of elevated capital spending. Taken together, supportive monetary policy, resilient corporate earnings and continued optimism around long-term technological innovation outweighed macro and policy-related doubts, allowing US equities to finish the quarter higher.

While AI remains a central theme, markets broadened in November, with value and non-AI stocks leading the way. We would welcome if this marked a shift in market leadership going forward. Accommodative monetary policy and anticipated fiscal stimulus in 2026 from the “One Big Beautiful Bill,” which should support overall economic and earnings growth, fueled expectations for broadening equity participation beyond AI investment beneficiaries. Additionally, hyperscalers’ rising capital intensity challenged one of the market’s core growth assumptions. As large cloud and AI platforms signaled sustained or accelerating capital expenditures to fund data centers, chips and power infrastructure, investors began to reassess near-term free cash flow trajectories and return profiles for these companies.

Large-cap stocks led in Q4, particularly value stocks, though growth narrowly finished ahead for the year. Within the S&P 500® Index, sector performance was mixed. Health care and communication services were notable leaders, posting strong gains. In contrast, all the other sectors had modest gains or declines. Real estate and utilities stocks detracted from performance, reflecting sensitivity to interest rate expectations.

Performance Discussion

The portfolio returned 2.21% in Q4 and finished the year up 10.03% (investor class). Over the past three years, the portfolio has generated a ~10% annualized total return while generating a current yield that was meaningfully higher than that of the broader US equity market. As a reminder, this portfolio—primarily comprised of dividend-paying stocks—is designed to balance income and capital appreciation. By participating in equity market upside, compounding wealth ahead of inflation and delivering a yield superior to broad US equities, the portfolio is fulfilling its objective.

In Q4, strength was broad-based across income categories and sectors, with the exception of our Bond Proxy holdings, which are largely utilities and real estate companies, and a Dividend Recovery holding—our sole position in that income bucket. Our top Q4 contributors were Lam Research (LRCX), Merck (MRK) and Wells Fargo (WFC). Lam Research, a global leader in wafer fabrication equipment used in the production of semiconductors, is continuing to see strong demand for its chipmaking tools supported by the current massive buildout of data centers running AI applications. We purchased Lam in April near the depths of the post-Liberation Day market selloff. As value investors, conditions of fear and uncertainty are fertile ground for creating attractive long-term buying opportunities. Few areas of the market were under greater pressure to start the year than semiconductors & semiconductor equipment stocks. We had been researching Lam since 2023, so we knew the company well and were able to act quickly when the stock plunged. At our initial purchase, Lam was selling for ~$60, almost 50% below its July 2024 highs. Lam is one of the key global suppliers of chip equipment serving the memory (NAND and DRAM) and foundry/logic markets. The financial condition is rock solid as it has a net cash balance sheet, and the company returns 100% of earnings to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends. Shares were selling for a 17X P/E on cyclically depressed earnings at the time of initial purchase, which we believed was an attractive valuation given the compounding nature of the business.

Shares of Merck, a health care solutions company, soared in November when health care stocks surged amid a rotation to cheaper laggards. The stock had gotten short shrift over the past year due to concerns it doesn’t have enough in the pipeline to offset blockbuster oncology drug Keytruda coming off patent in 2028. Merck has also garnered little respect for the multitude of deals it has completed over the past five years. Instead, the market has focused on the near-term headwinds in Gardasil, a vaccine for human papillomarivus (HPV). A narrative has formed that the company’s innovation engine is broken, and Merck is unlikely to navigate its way out of an eventual earnings headwind. Though recent Gardasil setbacks have weighed on sentiment, the overarching issue for shareholders remains the success of the company’s late-stage pipeline. Merck seems to be getting little credit from investors for the dozens of programs it has in clinical development, despite having several solid and large new product opportunities. Additionally, the company’s strong balance sheet and robust free cash flow provide it multiple options for future partnerships and acquisitions, besides return of capital to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases.

Wells Fargo, the fourth-largest US bank by assets, was one of our top performers in 2025 as the unprecedented $1.95 trillion asset cap that was imposed on the bank by the Federal Reserve in 2018 following its fake accounts scandal was lifted in June 2025 after the bank met the remainder of its regulatory requirements. CEO and Chairman Charlie Scharf, who joined Wells in 2019 after prior stints as CEO of Visa and Bank of New York Mellon, has overseen a culture change focused on accountability and the buildout of a new risk and control infrastructure. The lifting of the asset cap eliminates regulatory costs tied to the asset cap and allows the bank to return to “offense” as it seeks to drive higher returns on capital and rebuild competitiveness across business segments. In its first post-asset cap quarter, the bank delivered better-than-expected earnings per share growth and guided to a 17%–18% return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) versus 15% previously. We continue to find Wells’ return of capital (share buybacks and dividends), clean credit profile and valuation attractive.

Our biggest detractors were Baxter International (BAX), Genuine Parts (GPC) and PayPal Holdings (PYPL). Shares of health care company Baxter International fell after the company reported weak Q3 results in which organic revenue growth came in below expectations, reduced its guidance for Q4 and cut its dividend. The weakness was relatively broad-based across segments. Baxter’s multiyear restructuring effort that entailed selling several non-core operations to transform itself into a more profitable growth company has yet to bear fruit. The new CEO, who was appointed in July, communicated his focus on stabilizing underperforming businesses, strengthening the balance sheet and driving a culture of continuous improvement and efficiency. By reducing the dividend, the company is prioritizing deleveraging and investing in innovation. While Baxter’s cheap valuation is compelling, we sold our position as the stock no longer fits our income objectives after the company significantly cut the dividend.

Genuine Parts is a global distributor of automotive and industrial replacement parts. The stock declined roughly 10%, giving back a portion of its year-to-date gains. While sales growth improved—led by US company-owned NAPA stores—the growth was primarily price-driven rather than volume-driven, reinforcing concerns about soft underlying demand in both the automotive and industrial markets. We have held GPC since December 2024. It meets each of our three margin-of-safety criteria while offering an attractive 3.4% dividend yield. From a business economics perspective, the company consistently converts a high proportion of earnings into cash, and both segments generate solid returns on invested capital. The automotive business is steadier than the more cyclical industrial segment, as auto parts demand is largely tied to non-discretionary repair and maintenance. Growth in automotive also benefits from the company’s focus on DIFM (do-it-for-me), which continues to take share from DIY (do-it-yourself). The industrial segment appears to be near a cyclical trough following a multiyear contraction in US industrial production, with US PMIs below 50 in all but two months since 2023. We believe these dynamics provide multiple levers for improving performance from here, supported by potential capital allocation actions that could enhance the company’s otherwise modest organic growth profile. GPC is conservatively financed, with liquidity supported by ongoing cash generation. At the time of our initial purchase, the shares traded at approximately 13X forward earnings—well below peer valuations and, more importantly, at a significant discount to the company’s historical averages.

PayPal is the original e-commerce payments platform with years of experience in the marketplace, a large trove of customer data, a first-class risk engine and embedded consumer and merchant trust. Presently, PayPal has over 200 million monthly active accounts globally and executes 26 billion network transactions per year, which equates to $1.7 trillion of payments volumes. Though branded payment volumes growth has been steady, and transaction margins have expanded, investors want to see growth accelerate. The payments space is highly competitive, and PayPal’s place in the future is by no means a given despite its considerable incumbency advantages. The current CEO, who joined in 2023, is reinvigorating the company after prior management was unfocused, made poor acquisitions and underinvested. Shares currently sell for just 10X next year’s expected earnings. We believe this is a highly attractive valuation for a business with above-average—and improving—unit economics, a strong balance sheet and consistent free cash flow. While we wait for stabilization of market share and a return to higher growth rates, we should have plenty of free cash flow pointed back at us in the form of share repurchases and dividends.

For the full year, our top contributors were aforementioned Lam Research, Ryanair Holdings (RYAAY) and Philip Morris International (PM). Ryanair is an Ireland-based low-cost airline, which we classify as a Dividend Growth holding. Strong intra-Europe travel demand and restrained industry capacity are supporting the airline’s higher fares. While Ryanair uses hedges on fuel costs, the drop in oil prices is an additional tailwind for margins. Over the past 20 years, Ryanair’s low- cost, high on-time, high-efficiency model has helped it to take share from inefficient legacy and state-sponsored carriers. Industry consolidation, capacity growth that is below demand growth and a lack of overlap on key routes have contributed to its pricing power, strong returns on equity and free cash flow generation. Much of that free cash is returned to shareholders. In addition to a quarterly dividend, which was instituted in 2023, Ryanair is an opportunistic buyer of its own stock. Over the last decade, the company has retired 20% of its share count.

Philip Morris has had success transitioning its product portfolio away from traditional cigarettes—a category in steady decline—by focusing on potentially less harmful smoke-free products that offer superior growth potential. Its smoke-free product platform is led by IQOS, a heat-not-burn tobacco alternative, and Zyn nicotine pouches. Since its 2022 acquisition of Zyn maker Swedish Match, popularity of Zyn pouches has surged, helped by free advertising from social media influencers and Philip Morris’ ability to leverage its global scale and distribution. We believe Philip Morris still has significant runway for growth in the US—the world’s most profitable nicotine market and one that makes up less than 10% of PM’s revenues. Looking at Philip Morris through our margin of safety criteria, the business trades for an undemanding valuation and has extraordinary business economics and a strong credit profile.

On the downside, our biggest detractors were Cable One (CABO), Diageo (DEO) and Baxter International, which was previously discussed. Our investment case in Cable One, a broadband provider in rural markets, which we sold in September, had been predicated on Cable One’s ability to grow subscribers given its premium offering and low penetration; however, subscriber trends meaningfully weakened during our holding period. Tepid household formation in a sluggish housing market, fixed wireless and fiber competition taking market share and execution issues rolling out a new billing system were among the key factors. While its valuation indicated a cheap security based on a variety of scenarios, its financial condition is now impeding on the business and leaves management no room to operate the business strategically if conditions deteriorate further.

Diageo is the largest spirits company in the world by revenue, with over 200 brands to choose from. Diageo and other spirits makers are contending with a mix of cyclical and structural demand headwinds. Growth has normalized after a COVID-induced bounce, and consumers have been trading down to cheaper value alternatives, which is a headwind for Diageo’s premium brands. Additionally, changing consumer preferences, GLP-1 weight loss drugs and aging demographics are sapping demand. Tariff uncertainty further clouds Diageo’s profits growth outlook. In the near term, margin expansion will likely be constrained, but the company continues to generate meaningful free cash flow and return it to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Over the past five years, Diageo generated $15 billion in free cash flow and returned $17 billion to shareholders. Despite current growth challenges, Diageo remains a market leader, with strong brands and significant scale and distribution advantages. Shares sell for just 12X EV/EBIT (enterprise value to EBIT)—the cheapest since 2009—providing us, we believe, with a favorable risk/reward.

Portfolio Activity

Our largest new purchase was Houston-headquartered Prosperity BancShares (PB), the fifth-largest bank in Texas. We have followed the bank for almost 20 years, and it’s been run by CEO David Zalman for that entire time. Mr. Zalman is fanatical about expense management, credit quality and deposit gathering. The bank over time is a “consolidator of choice” for local Texas bank CEOs who want to retire and roll their equity into Prosperity. As one of its largest shareholders, Mr. Zalman runs Prosperity like a true owner-operator. As such, he is perfectly content doing nothing for a few years and waiting for acquisition opportunities to come to him. Despite having strong credit quality and being well capitalized, the stock has significantly trailed its peers over the past few years and is now trading near the bottom of its long-run valuation range on various valuations measures. Given its cheap valuation and strong balance sheet, the reward to risk appears favorable for this high-quality regional bank.

Another notable purchase was Union Pacific (UNP), the largest freight railroad in the US. Transports are cyclical and generally of lower quality due to capital intensity and less pricing power in downturns when excess capacity exists. As a result, there are very few business models in the space which possess a wide moat. UNP is unique in that despite its cyclicality and capital intensity, the business has pricing power and scale, allowing it to sustain high margins even in downturns. UNP owns one of the most valuable transport assets globally, with an irreplaceable, east–west rail network spanning 52,000 miles. UNP’s stock has lagged due to a prolonged freight recession, weak intermodal volumes amid depressed trucking rates and investor skepticism around near-term volume growth. Uncertainty around the timing, concessions and ultimate benefits of the proposed Norfolk Southern merger has also capped sentiment. The main evolution in the industry in the last 20 years is the operational shift to precision scheduled railroading (PSR). The key theme of PSR is “efficiency,” which is created by operating fewer, longer and heavier trains on a simplified network to increase asset utilization by decreasing dwell time and increasing speed. PSR has structurally improved efficiency, asset utilization and operating ratios, positioning UNP as arguably the highest quality business in rail. UNP generates attractive economics with mid-teens returns on invested capital, steady capex and strong free cash flow. The balance sheet is resilient, and even post-merger, leverage is expected to remain manageable, with ample cash flow to de-lever while maintaining dividends. Shares trade below historical earnings multiples, despite improving operations and a potential generational merger opportunity.

Besides our sale of Baxter International, we also exited LKQ, a vehicle parts distributor. While LKQ’s low valuation suggests little downside risk, we have become less optimistic about repairable claims inflecting higher in the near term.

Perspective

There is no shortage of reasons investors may be concerned about risks to US equities in 2026. These include elevated valuations following three consecutive years of strong market returns; high concentration within market-cap-weighted large-cap indices; concerns about Federal Reserve independence; political uncertainty surrounding upcoming US midterm elections; ongoing geopolitical risks (including Ukraine, Iran, Venezuela and Greenland); and renewed inflation and interest rate risks stemming from tariffs, fiscal stimulus and reduced immigration. We can appreciate these concerns; however, as bottom-up value investors, we see a very different opportunity set beneath the surface. Many stocks in our value universe have not fully participated in the past three years’ bull market and continue to trade at attractive relative valuations. Moreover, growth may broaden across the economy as policy stimulus supports more widespread economic participation, potentially benefiting many of our portfolio companies. Separately, the increasing application of AI has the potential to drive productivity gains across industries, helping narrow the gap between the “haves” and “have-nots” after a period in which a relatively small group of technology-focused companies has garnered supernormal profits growth.

The portfolio continues to be tilted toward high-quality large-cap names, selling at undemanding valuations, with attractive income characteristics. We can’t predict the markets or economic trends. What we can control is the execution of our process, and we believe the characteristics of the portfolio today demonstrate our continued execution of our investment discipline.

Free Cash Flow is a measure of financial performance calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures. Net Cash is a figure that is reported on a company's financial statements. It is calculated by subtracting a company's total liabilities from its total cash. Price-to-Earnings (P/E) is a valuation ratio of a company's current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Return on Capital (ROC) is a measure of how effectively a company uses the money (borrowed or owned) invested in its operations. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is a measure of how well a company generates cash flow relative to capital invested in the business. Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE) is a profitability ratio that measures the amount of net income as a percentage of shareholders’ tangible equity minus intangible assets and preferred stock. Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EV/EBIT) is a valuation multiple defined as an enterprise value (EV) divided by earnings before interest and tax (EBIT). References to “better, safer, cheaper” are based on views of a security’s Margin of Safety. Margin of safety, a concept developed by Benjamin Graham, is the difference between the market price and the estimated intrinsic value of a business. A large margin of safety may help guard against permanent capital loss and improve the probability of capital appreciation. data.title: Hydreight Technologies: High Growth And Massive Asymmetry
data.description:
Investment thesis

Hydreight Technologies (NURS:CA) (HYDTF) is a story of high growth through a mix of segments with concepts already proven in other industries, but focused on digital health, such as the "Uber of nurses" and recently the launch of the "digital healthcare Shopify", through its new VSDHOne that was launched at the end of Q3 2025 and that in 2026 will experience the ramp up and consequent operating leverage because the development costs have already been covered and the company is vertically integrated now.

However, there's a kind of discount in the stock due to lack of confidence in future execution. I think the numbers speak for themselves, with impressive 2026 guidance recently released, so sooner or later this discount should disappear.

Two legacy segments

Hydreight Technologies could be considered as a conglomerate of three totally different business models, but related to the health industry, especially the digital focus. On the one hand, there's the platform for nurses and health professionals to offer mobile services like what Uber would be but with doctors and nurses, with Hydreight being the intermediary between them and patients seeking home medical services and allowing nurses to operate legally without having to build their own backend from scratch. This concept is very good and works, with a 63% increase in new sign-ups, but it's not the company's main bet.

On the other hand, there's the pharmacy and physical store channel, where prescriptions and product orders from clinical partners and Hydreight's own network of nurses are delivered. In simple terms, every time a treatment is prescribed through the platform, the supply chain is controlled by Hydreight and thus seeks to integrate as vertically as possible, something important now that we're seeing how the narrative of AI and software isn't too positive.

What makes these two segments attractive is their recurring nature and the growth they're experiencing, since between physical clinic orders and the nurse network, the growth was approximately 72% year over year.

The new big growth bet

Although, as I mentioned before, the two other business segments are interesting and growing, the company's big bet is the new VSDHOne, a model that seeks to create a digital health infrastructure so that networks of independent nurses, clinics or doctors can connect to a ready to operate digital health environment without having to build it from scratch. Kind of like what Shopify does: instead of being a website to sell something, it provides the environment in which sellers can create their website to sell something.

This is a big thing and I consider that the value added is very high, since it offers quick access to a functional page with online booking, lab testing, a physician network, prescription issuance, payment processing, prescription fulfillment, and most important of all, the regulatory compliance, an aspect in which the company has invested more because it's the crucial point for independent doctors to truly expand across the 50 states of the country with as little friction as possible.

In other words, while some telehealth providers are quite well-known, such as Hims & Hers (HIMS), Teladoc (TDOC) or LifeMD (LFMD), what's different about Hydreight, through VSDHOne, is that it's trying to become the rails (the operating system) behind digital healthcare delivery without being the visible face. That distinction is important because infrastructure models tend to be less marketing dependent.

Valuation

With the official launch of VSDHOne in the Q3 2025 and expansion ramping up, all that remains is to start seeing that growth reach the bottom line, as development costs have already been covered and any incremental sales would begin operating leverage. Meaning they don't need to invest more to grow more.

Based on the latest guidance, strong revenue growth is expected through 2026, driven by continued growth in legacy segments and monetization of VSDHOne. Although there's no exact guidance for fiscal year 2025 (which is already completed and we are only waiting for the official figures to be reported) for 2026 the initial guidance is approximately $150 million in revenue, an extraordinary figure considering that fiscal year 2024 (without VSDHOne) closed with $16 million in sales alone.

Considering this guidance, I will be more conservative and estimate that $150 million in sales will be reached until 2027 and from there sales will grow 20% annually. Additionally, I expect the free cash flow margin to expand to 15%, somewhat below what comparables like Shopify (SHOP) or Uber (UBER) earn. That means that within five years the company would be earning just over $30 million in cash flow and applying a multiple of 20 times, reasonable for a company growing so much, the enterprise value would be approximately $665 million, that is, more than three times the current value. Why's it so cheap? I will comment on it below.

Risks

I think one of the reasons the stock is apparently trading so cheaply is because there's some uncertainty around the numbers, management and execution going forward. The Q3 earnings call introduced narrative changes that understandably created skepticism among investors. Revenue recognition moved from order placement to order receipt and at the same time, management recognized that GLP-1 costs have decreased dramatically and that discounts were offered to accelerate the migration to VSDHOne. When metrics change midstream, even if they are justified, the market tends to assume the worst and at the time Q3 was reported it was thought that this change in revenue recognition was to cover up the slowdown in growth, but we have already seen that it seems that this was not the case.

Also, there's the risk of concentration around GLP-1 treatments for weight loss. While we don't know what percentage of current revenue has been linked to GLP-1 compounded medications, we know it's relevant. So the recent controversies surrounding Hims & Hers, Novo Nordisk and the FDA introduce a risk that Hydreight's customers will also see restrictions on sales of this medical compound and this will lead to reduced growth.

The bottom line

The story seems promising, the numbers have corroborated the success so far and 2026 should be the year of consolidation of the concept. I think this opportunity exists because there's a sort of "distrust discount" to the stock due to skepticism towards execution moving forward and transparency of management. Regardless of whether it's justified or not (I believe is not), I understand why this discount exists. In short, the stock is extremely cheap and if over time they continue to execute as before, trust could be regained and the valuation should be adjusted positively.
